nodes.statement: 7.6 million people were waiting for NHS treatment as of June 2024, up from 2.4 million in March 2010, with over 300,000 waiting more than a year
A&E 4-hour target performance fell from 96.6% in 2011 to 74.5% in 2024, and the 18-week treatment target has not been met since 2016
NHS productivity is approximately 11% lower than 2019 levels, despite a 17% increase in hospital staff over the same period
13% of hospital beds are occupied by patients waiting for social care, a phenomenon known as bed-blocking that costs the NHS billions annually

The Darzi Review found the UK has spent £37 billion less on health capital than comparable OECD countries since 2010, while the NHS buildings maintenance backlog reached £15.9 billion in 2024/25, more than double the real-terms cost a decade earlier
One in five NHS staff are foreign nationals, 27% of nurses and 50% of secondary-care doctors trained abroad
20% of primary care estate predates 1948, and the NHS is estimated to be 15 years behind the private sector on technology adoption
Hospital nursing staff increased 35% between 2009 and 2023, while community nursing fell 5% over the same period
2.8 million people are economically inactive due to long-term sickness, and each person returned to work generates approximately £25,000 per year in economic output plus reduced benefits

The IPPR estimates that achieving the NHS Elective Recovery Plan would generate total benefits worth £73 billion over five years from 2022 to 2027, comprising £18 billion from people returning to paid work, £55 billion from increased unpaid production, and £14 billion in healthcare cost savings
Every £1 spent on public health prevention interventions saves between £4 and £14 in subsequent treatment costs
19% of the adult social care workforce are non-UK nationals, and the overseas care worker recruitment route was closed by Labour in July 2025
The RCN predicts nursing numbers will more than halve by 2029 without intervention in recruitment and retention
Between 15,000 and 23,000 doctors leave the NHS annually according to BMA workforce data
Approximately one million people are waiting for mental health services within the NHS
Waiting lists can be cleared within one parliamentary term without funding well beyond current manifesto commitments
nodes.verdict: NOT ACHIEVABLE in 5 years. Darzi calls it unlikely. IFS says reducing to pre-pandemic levels is highly unlikely within the next parliament. Labour's 40,000 extra appointments represents only a 2% increase on 145 million annual outpatient appointments.

nodes.statement: NHS funding at manifesto-pledged levels is sufficient to deliver the Long Term Workforce Plan and restore performance standards
nodes.verdict: SYSTEMIC UNDERFUNDING. All three main parties' pledges would make the next four years the tightest in NHS history. A £20-23bn gap exists between manifesto commitments and LTWP funding requirements, which would require productivity growth at 3-4x historic rates to close.

nodes.statement: The NHS Long Term Workforce Plan can deliver its staffing targets without continued reliance on overseas recruitment for the next 15 years
nodes.verdict: WORKFORCE PLAN IS NECESSARY BUT INSUFFICIENT WITHOUT OVERSEAS RECRUITMENT IN THE INTERIM. Doubling medical school places takes 10-15 years to produce trained doctors. Labour has simultaneously closed the care worker overseas route while the workforce plan depends on that route continuing.

nodes.statement: Social care reform will be delivered by the current government despite every government for the past 25 years promising and failing to do so
nodes.verdict: NO PARTY HAS A CREDIBLE SOCIAL CARE PLAN WITH ADEQUATE FUNDING. The Dilnot Commission recommended a costs cap in 2011. Conservatives legislated for it, then delayed twice. Labour committed to a National Care Service with no roadmap or interim funding.

nodes.statement: Technology and AI adoption can close the NHS productivity gap without addressing the underlying capital investment shortfall
nodes.verdict: TECHNOLOGY IS NECESSARY BUT REQUIRES CAPITAL INVESTMENT FIRST. Technology deployed on crumbling infrastructure does not work. The £37bn capital shortfall must be addressed before AI in diagnostics can deliver at scale.

nodes.statement: Shifting NHS spending from hospitals to community care and prevention would be more cost-effective than the current hospital-dominated model
nodes.verdict: SHIFT TO PREVENTION IS THE SINGLE MOST COST-EFFECTIVE REFORM. Every serious analysis agrees. But it requires sustained investment over 10-20 years before savings materialise. Every government wants the savings but won't make the investment.

nodes.statement: Expanding private healthcare through tax relief would reduce pressure on the NHS at a system level
nodes.verdict: NOT A SYSTEM-LEVEL SOLUTION. The same doctors and nurses work in both systems. Tax relief subsidises private care while drawing workforce away from the NHS. No evidence that expanding private healthcare reduces NHS waiting lists at system level.

nodes.statement: Every major party's manifesto funding commitment would deliver the tightest NHS funding settlement in history, with a gap of £20-23 billion between commitments and workforce plan requirements
Labour's immigration policy of closing the overseas care worker route directly contradicts its own NHS workforce plan which depends on overseas recruitment for at least 15 years
The total additional NHS and social care funding needed above current manifesto commitments is approximately £20-25 billion per year
Social care failure is the single largest driver of the A&E crisis, with 13% of hospital beds blocked by patients who cannot be discharged to adequate social care

The NHS is a system designed in 1948 running on infrastructure partly predating 1948, with a workforce plan that depends on overseas recruitment, in a society getting sicker due to factors the NHS does not control
Fund the NHS at minimum 3.5% real-terms annual growth, the historic average and the minimum the Long Term Workforce Plan requires, rather than the 0.9-1.5% pledged in manifestos
Clear the £37 billion capital maintenance backlog over ten years at approximately £3.7 billion per year, prioritising buildings and digital infrastructure
Ring-fence a minimum of £1 billion per year for public health prevention, given that every £1 in prevention saves £4-14 in treatment costs
Fund social care at minimum £5 billion per year additional, implement a Fair Pay Agreement at £12 per hour minimum, and deliver free personal care for those with the highest needs
Maintain overseas NHS and social care recruitment routes during the 15-year domestic workforce training transition rather than closing them prematurely

Shift 20% of hospital budgets to community-based multidisciplinary teams over ten years, with each team covering approximately 30,000 people with integrated GP, nursing, mental health, and social care services
Treat the 2.8 million economically inactive due to long-term sickness as a joint NHS-DWP economic emergency with dedicated return-to-work rehabilitation capacity
nodes.source_loc: Innovation 5: Long-Term Sickness as Economic Emergency

nodes.statement: Implement a single integrated digital health and care record accessible across NHS, social care, mental health, and community services, following Estonia's e-health model
nodes.source_loc: Innovation 4: Integrated Health and Care Records

nodes.statement: Implement the NHS Long Term Workforce Plan with 92,000 more nurses and 28,000 more doctors by end of parliament, while reducing managers by 5,500
Provide an extra £3.7 billion per year for social care and cap individual care costs
Invest £3.4 billion in NHS AI and technology and deliver the New Hospitals Programme
Provide 40,000 extra NHS appointments per week, deliver the Long Term Workforce Plan, and create a National Care Service with a Fair Pay Agreement for social care workers
Reform the Mental Health Act, deploy the NHS App as the front door to services, and shift towards prevention with a 10-year plan
Invest an extra £8.4 billion per year in health and social care by 2028/29, recruit 8,000 more GPs, and guarantee cancer treatment within 62 days
Deliver free personal care, a carer's minimum wage at £2 per hour above standard, an extra £1 billion per year for public health, and establish a Royal College of Care Workers

Invest £20 billion in free personal care, restore public health budgets with an extra £1.5 billion per year, and address social determinants of health by ending the distinction between health and social care
Invest an extra £17 billion per year in the NHS, eliminate waiting lists within two years, and provide tax relief on private healthcare to reduce NHS demand
